name: Promontory Point
short_description: Public BLM rockhounding near Bear Lake with easy access to agate and chalcedony specimens. No permit required - collect up to 25 lbs per day on this scenic northern Utah site.
description:
Promontory Point sits on BLM-managed land in Box Elder County near the Bear Lake area, offering accessible rockhounding opportunities for collectors of all skill levels. The site features moderate rolling terrain at 4,729 feet elevation with sparse sagebrush vegetation typical of Utah's high desert. This location is known for producing colorful agate nodules, chalcedony pieces, and quartz specimens that weathered out of volcanic and sedimentary host rock.

The geology here reflects the Basin and Range province, where Tertiary-age volcanic activity and subsequent hydrothermal alteration created silica-rich deposits. Agate and chalcedony formed in cavities and fractures within these volcanic rocks over millions of years, with weathering and erosion exposing specimens on slopes and in wash drainages today.

Collecting is straightforward and beginner-friendly. Most productive areas lie on gentle slopes and in small washes where material weathered naturally from outcrop. The moderate slope allows hikers to cover ground efficiently, and parking is accessible via improved dirt roads. This is an excellent site for families and new rockhounders looking for hands-on experience without technical climbing or special permits.

Bring plenty of water, sturdy hiking boots, and a backpack or collecting bag. The high desert sun can be intense even in cooler months. Plan to spend 2-3 hours systematically walking slopes and checking washes for loose specimens. This site works well as a half-day outing from nearby Brigham City.
history: Promontory Point lies within the Basin and Range physiographic province, where Miocene-age volcanic activity deposited silica-rich volcanic rocks and associated hydrothermal fluids that precipitated agate, chalcedony, and quartz in fractures and cavities over time. Natural weathering and erosion over the past several million years have exposed these secondary silicate minerals on the surface, making them accessible to modern rockhounders.
tips: Search gentle slopes facing washes and small drainages - these areas concentrate weathered specimens that have rolled downhill. Look for translucent to opaque agate nodules in cream, tan, orange, and reddish colors, typically 1-4 inches across. Use a hand lens to spot banding patterns that distinguish true agate from common quartz. Bring a sturdy backpack and plan to walk uphill from parking to survey fresh exposures; avoid retracing the same ground other collectors have already worked.
terrain_notes: Moderate rolling high-desert terrain at 4,729 feet elevation with sparse low sagebrush, grass, and scattered rocks. Slopes are walkable but uneven with scattered loose stones requiring sturdy footwear. Parking to collecting areas typically 0.5 to 1.5 miles on foot. No significant cliffs or drop-offs, but terrain is broken enough to demand attention to footing. Minimal shade available.
best_season: October - April. Summer heat at this high-desert elevation can exceed 85 degrees with intense sun and minimal shade. Fall and spring offer comfortable temperatures (40-60 degrees) and stable weather. Winter is possible but can bring snow and muddy roads. Late spring wildflowers add to the experience.
written_directions: From Brigham City, take US 89 north toward Logan for approximately 8 miles. Turn east on the signed road toward Promontory Point (this area is marked on most maps as the Promontory historic site region). Follow the improved dirt road east for roughly 4 miles, watching for BLM rockhounding access points and small pullouts on the north and south sides of the road. Park at any designated pullout and walk upslope or into nearby washes. GPS coordinates 41.226854, -112.417667 mark the general area; use a mapping app to locate current access points, as BLM road conditions may vary seasonally.
road_conditions: Improved dirt road, well-maintained but uneven surface. Passenger vehicles suitable in dry conditions; high clearance not required. Road may be muddy or rutted after rain.
parking_notes: Small dirt pullouts and turnouts along the access road; no formal parking lot. Space for 3-5 vehicles at each pullout. No amenities. Park completely off the road to avoid blocking access.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or powered equipment. Hand tools only (pick, shovel, screen). Collect loose material and weathered specimens; do not break bedrock or outcrop. Leave the site as you found it.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Hand tools only
Do not break bedrock or outcrop
nearest_services: Brigham City, approximately 10 miles southwest via US 89. Full services including gas, food, water, and lodging available.
accessibility_notes: Relatively easy terrain for most hikers. Uneven rocky slopes with scattered loose stones require sturdy hiking boots. No wheelchair access to collecting areas. Parking pullouts are accessible by standard passenger vehicle on dry-condition roads. Moderate uphill walking (0.5-1.5 miles) to reach prime collecting zones. Not suitable for those with limited mobility or inability to walk uneven terrain.

faq.question: What types of agates can I find at Promontory Point, Utah?
faq.answer: Promontory Point yields colorful agate nodules typically in cream, tan, orange, and reddish hues, ranging from 1 to 4 inches in diameter. Many specimens show banding patterns or translucent edges characteristic of chalcedony agate. You will also find solid chalcedony pieces and quartz fragments. The best specimens are found weathered loose on slopes rather than embedded in bedrock.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Promontory Point in Box Elder County?
faq.answer: No permit is required. Promontory Point sits on BLM public land where personal recreational collecting is allowed under FLPMA regulations. You can collect up to 25 lbs of agate, chalcedony, and quartz per day at no cost. Commercial collection and motorized excavation are prohibited.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound near Brigham City at Promontory Point?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal. Summer temperatures exceed 85 degrees with intense desert sun and minimal shade, making daytime collecting uncomfortable. Fall and spring offer mild temperatures between 40-60 degrees with stable weather. Winter is possible but roads may become muddy after snow melt. Late spring also brings wildflower displays.

faq.question: How difficult is the hike to find agates at Promontory Point, and is it family-friendly?
faq.answer: The site is beginner and family-friendly with easy terrain. Most collecting areas are 0.5 to 1.5 miles from parking on uneven but walkable slopes. Children as young as 6 can participate with adult supervision. Sturdy hiking boots are essential due to scattered loose rocks. The moderate slope is not steep, making it suitable for families with basic fitness levels.

faq.question: What tools and supplies should I bring for rockhounding at Promontory Point?
faq.answer: Bring a hand pick or small pry bar for extracting specimens from soil, a sturdy backpack or collecting bag, and a hand lens to identify agate banding. Most important: bring 2-3 liters of water per person, as the high-desert site offers no shade or water sources. Wear sturdy hiking boots, a wide-brimmed hat, and sunscreen. A small screening tool helps sift material in productive washes.

faq.question: Can I bring my dog rockhounding at Promontory Point, Utah?
faq.answer: Yes, dogs are welcome on BLM land at Promontory Point. Keep your dog under control and bring plenty of water for both you and your pet, as the high-desert terrain offers no shade or natural water sources. The uneven rocky terrain can be tough on paw pads, so consider dog boots if your dog is not acclimated to desert hiking. Stay aware of potential wildlife encounters.

meta_title: Promontory Point Rockhounding - Utah Agate & Chalcedony
meta_description: Find agate, chalcedony, and quartz at Promontory Point on BLM land near Brigham City, Utah. No permit required, 25 lbs per day limit. Family-friendly rockhounding.
